heading: NOTE AL SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CXXXV
card_summary: This chunk consists of densely annotated footnotes (notes 140–185) to Campanini's introductory essay, documenting Zorzi's sources and parallel passages drawn primarily from the Problemata and Declarationes. Topics covered include: the Valley of Hinnom/Gehenna and Judas's death; the empty tomb of the Virgin Mary and Rachel's tomb on the Ephrath road; the hospitality of Abraham at Hebron (with the hospital distributing thousands of loaves daily cited from multiple manuscript witnesses including Ms. Yahuda Var. 24 and Ms. Piancastelli VII 57); the Dead Sea/Mare Mortuum (its poisonous waters, asphalt, salt, and the apples of Sodom); the mountain of Temptation; Mount Sinai's spiritual rather than geographical proximity to Jerusalem (citing Galatians 4:25 and Psalm 133); the Ezra/Assyrian Hebrew script question and Sinai inscriptions; and the full bibliographic details of the 1525 Venice editio princeps of De harmonia mundi and its subsequent Paris editions (1543–1546, 1564).
essay:
# Notes to the Introductory Essay (pp. 135–139): Annotations 140–185

## Overview and Function

This chunk is entirely apparatus: Campanini's footnote sequence running from note 140 to note 185, appended to his introductory essay on Zorzi's life and works. The notes serve three interrelated purposes: (1) they supply parallel passages from Zorzi's other major works, above all the *Problemata* and the *Declarationes*, to demonstrate the consistency and development of Zorzi's ideas; (2) they introduce manuscript evidence from the *Declarationes* (Ms. Yahuda Var. 24 and Ms. Piancastelli VII 57) alongside printed witnesses; and (3) they anchor Zorzi's statements in the secondary literature, citing Maillard (1991), Secret (1974, 1975, 1985), Ripberger (1962), and others.

## The Valley of Hinnom, Judas, and the Field of Blood (notes 140–141)

Note 140 cites *Problemata* 2,6,287 on the Valley of Hinnom (Gehenna), which Jeremiah was commanded to rename *polyandrium* ("burial ground of the dead"), identified as the site where Judas hanged himself. This typological geography — Old Testament curse-site overlaid with New Testament betrayal — is characteristic of Zorzi's method of reading landscape as theological sign.

## Rachel's Tomb and the Virgin Mary's Empty Tomb (notes 144–147)

Note 145 quotes *Problemata* 4,9,496 on Mary's assumption:

> Igitur id Mariae praestitum fuisse non est ambigendum. Cuius rei potissimum indicium est quod mausoleum eius reperitur vacuum.

<cite>Problemata 4,9,496, cited in note 145, p. 136</cite>

The empty tomb is presented as the chief proof of Mary's bodily assumption. Note 147 adds the eyewitness dimension: Zorzi claims to have personally seen (*propriis oculis vidimus*) Rachel's tomb still standing on the Ephrath road from Jerusalem to Bethlehem, cross-referencing *De harmonia mundi* 2,7,6.

## Abraham's Hospitality at Hebron (notes 151–152)

Notes 151–152 are among the most texturally rich in the chunk. Note 151 quotes *Problemata* 1,3,110 at length on the Hospital of Saint Abraham in the Valley of Hebron, where Zorzi claims personal presence and consumption of food (*novimus, vidimus et ex eis comedimus*), noting that twelve or thirteen thousand loaves were distributed daily along with meat. Note 152 then presents two manuscript variants of the *Declarationes*:

> Ipse fuit vir pius et misericors et libenter pauperes et peregrinos hospitabatur. Unde et in memoriam huius magnae hospitalitatis, quia ipse Abraham utebatur erga peregrinos, erectum est hospitale magnum in valle Hebron, quod usque hodie manet, in quo quotidie distribuuntur peregrinis et egenis quinque millia panum cum carne ipsis proportionata. Et vocatur Hospitale sancti Abrahae.

<cite>Declarationes, Ms. Yahuda Var. 24, f. 93v, cited in note 152, p. 136</cite>

The Piancastelli manuscript gives a variant reading with the same substance but slight verbal differences. The discrepancy between five thousand and twelve-to-thirteen thousand loaves between the *Declarationes* manuscripts and the *Problemata* is noted implicitly by Campanini's parallel citation. Campanini refers readers to Secret 1975, p. 99, n. 71.

## The Dead Sea / Mare Mortuum (notes 155–161)

Notes 155–160 form an extended dossier on the Dead Sea as a theater of divine wrath. Note 156 quotes *Problemata* 6,7,261 at length:

> Unde oritur mare Melach, vel asphaltum, et cur venenosum et letale est? Nonne a Iordane fluvio fit lacus ille in pentapoli Syriae, qui vulgo mare mortuum dicitur? [...] Nam poma in littore eius nascentia, et si pomi cortices habent, intus tamen plena sunt cinere vel pulvere.

<cite>Problemata 6,7,261, cited in note 156, p. 137</cite>

The Hebrew etymology is given (*mare salis* from *Malach*), and the apples of Sodom — beautiful in appearance but filled with ash — serve as a moral-cosmological emblem. Note 157 (citing Geyer 1898) and note 158 (citing Monneret de Villard 1950) adduce corroborating ancient and medieval pilgrimage accounts. Note 160 provides a *Declarationes* passage (Ms. Yahuda Var. 24, f. 39v) on the ongoing signs of divine anger:

> Et in signum irae divinae adhuc super illos effervescentis terra regionis illius est penitus infructuosa velut cinis, aqua amara, fructus sunt pleni fumo, aves circumvolantes cadunt mortuae. Et qui vidit testimonium perhibuit.

<cite>Declarationes, Ms. Yahuda Var. 24, f. 39v, cited in note 160, p. 137–138</cite>

The ms. Piancastelli VII 57 variant is also given. The region of Sodom and Gomorrah is described in *Problemata* 6,9,333 as the "gate of Hell" (*porta inferni*).

## Mount Sinai and the Spiritual Topology of Jerusalem (notes 165–171)

Notes 165–171 address the Pauline claim (Galatians 4:25) that Sinai is "conjoined" to earthly Jerusalem. Zorzi insists — against literal geography — that the conjunction is one of *influxus* (spiritual influence), not physical contiguity, since the two sites are fifteen days' journey apart. *De harmonia mundi* 1,7,35 is quoted to this effect, with a parallel to Psalm 133:3 on the dew of Hermon descending on Zion despite eighty thousand paces of separation. Note 169 cites *Problemata* 1,9,488, which adds the Queen of Sheba passage (Matthew 12:42; Luke 11:31) into the same typological network. Note 171 (*Problemata* 4,4,172) confirms Zorzi's reading of Paul: "Quod de Ierusalem et Sina inferiori intelligi nequit."

## The Song of Songs, Moses at the Rock, and Ezra's Script (notes 172–184)

Note 173 (*Problemata* 5,1,67) raises the exegetical problem of the Beloved "leaping over mountains" (Canticles 2:8) — whether the mountains are Sinai and Horeb as two summits on the same base. Note 175 (*Problemata* 1,6,373) cites the rock where Moses stood to receive the vision of God's back (Exodus 33:21), with an eyewitness claim about a footprint-shaped indentation still visible.

Notes 176–184 turn to the question of Hebrew script reform. Note 176 mentions Bernardo Paltrinieri's letter to Cornelius Agrippa (December 1532) transmitting what is called the "alphabet of Ezra" (*alphabetum hebraicum, ut vocant, Esdrae*). Notes 177–181 situate this within rabbinic tradition (TB Sanhedrin 21b; *Sefer ha-Peli'ah*) and ancient sources (Diodorus Siculus, *Bibliotheca historica* 3,42; Cosmas Indicopleustes, *Topographia Christiana* 5), noting that Diodorus speaks of "unknown script" at Sinai while Cosmas specifies Hebrew. Note 184 gives *Problemata* 6,3,87 on the Hebrews' use of different characters before the Sinai theophany, suggesting that God may have given the Law written in Chaldean (Assyrian square) script. Campanini corrects Secret's proposed date of 1518 for a related publication (note 182), noting that the dedicatee Andrea Gritti was not yet Doge that year.

## The Editio Princeps of De Harmonia Mundi (note 185)

Note 185 provides the full title and colophon of the 1525 Venice first edition:

> Francisci Georgi veneti minoritanae familiae De harmonia mundi totius cantica tria [...] Venetiis in aedibus Bernardini de Vitalibus calcographi, An[no] D[omini] MDXXV, mense septembri.

<cite>Colophon of the editio princeps, cited in note 185, p. 139</cite>

The subsequent Paris edition of 1543 (with further issues in 1544, 1545, 1546) and a later Paris reprint retitled *Promptuarium rerum et theologicarum et philosophicarum* (J. Macé, 1564) are also recorded. Campanini notes the 2008 anastatica reprint by La Finestra (Lavis, TN) with a preface by C. Vasoli.

## Significance for Zorzi's Larger Project

The cumulative weight of these annotations reveals Zorzi's distinctive method: sacred geography is never merely physical but always carries a superimposed spiritual topology in which proximity and distance are measured by *influxus* rather than miles. The Dead Sea functions as a permanently legible sign of divine wrath; Hebron as a living monument to Abrahamic charity; Sinai as spiritually contiguous with Jerusalem despite literal separation. The script-reform notes connect Zorzi's interest in Kabbalah and Hebrew philology to the question of the very medium of divine revelation.
